segments.purgatorio-14-g1: Chi è costui introduces a question: Chi (who) + è (present indicative of essere) gives “Who is this one.” The relative che begins a clause describing him (’l = il, archaic elision). Cerchia is 3rd person singular present of cerchiare (“encircles”). Prima che + abbia dato uses a temporal subordinate with the present perfect subjunctive (abbia + past participle) because prima che triggers the subjunctive for an action temporally anterior/anticipated with respect to the speaker’s viewpoint; li here functions as an unstressed clitic referring to “him” (dative/indirect object: “to him”), and volo is literal “flight” used figuratively (“death has given him his flight/release”).
segments.purgatorio-14-g2: E apre li occhi a sua voglia e coverchia is coordinated to the previous clause: e apre (and opens) is present indicative, li occhi uses the archaic plural article li + noun, and a sua voglia means “at his will/as he pleases.” Coverchia is an archaic/poetic form meaning to cover/close (here likely a 3rd-person singular present “he covers [them]”); the form is poetical rather than modern standard Italian.
segments.purgatorio-14-g3: A single period marking the end of the preceding line/speech; there are no lexical items to analyze.
segments.purgatorio-14-g4: Non so is the simple present “I do not know.” Chi sia is an indirect question clause using the present subjunctive (sia) — typical in Italian after verbs of knowing/uncertainty.
segments.purgatorio-14-g5: Ma so begins the contrast “but I know.” Ch’e’ is the contracted che + è (poetic elision) introducing the content of that knowledge (“that he is”), and non è solo is the predicate “is not alone.” The contraction ch’e’ is a running-together typical of older/poetic Italian.
segments.purgatorio-14-g6: Domandal tu is an imperative/command form with an enclitic object: domandal = domandalo (ask him), with the subject pronoun tu placed after the verb (common in older/verse word order). The following che più li t’avvicini is a relative clause “you who approach nearer to him”: che = who; più = nearer/more; li is the clitic referring to “him” (dative), placed before the reflexive/object clitic t’ in the older clitic order; avvicini is present indicative (you approach).
segments.purgatorio-14-g7: E dolcemente is a simple coordination: e = and; dolcemente is an adverb meaning “gently/softly.”
segments.purgatorio-14-g8: Sì che parli introduces purpose/result: sì che = “so that,” and parli is present subjunctive (3rd person) expressing the wished-for result — “so that he may speak.”
segments.purgatorio-14-g9: Acco’lo is an archaic/poetic compound of accò (there/approach) + lo (him) meaning roughly “go to him” or “approach him there”; it is a short direction closing the injunction. The closing punctuation ends the quoted speech.
segments.purgatorio-14-g10: Così is an adverb meaning “thus/so,” linking this description to what precedes. due spirti is the noun phrase “two spirits” (archaic spelling spirti for modern spiriti), with due modifying spirti.
segments.purgatorio-14-g11: l’uno a l’altro chini literally 'the one bent to the other'; l’uno and l’altro are definite, and a l’ is the preposition + article contraction (a + il). chini is an adjectival past/participial form meaning 'bowed/bent' and agrees in number/gender with spirti.
segments.purgatorio-14-g12: ragionavan is the imperfect indicative 3rd plural 'they were conversing/discussing'. di me is the prepositional object 'about me', ivi is a locative adverb 'there', and a man dritta is a fixed idiom meaning 'on (the) right hand/side' (man = mano shortened).
segments.purgatorio-14-g13: poi 'then'; fer is an archaic past-form of fare (here functioning like fece/fecero) meaning 'made/caused' — contextually 'they turned' (i.e. caused their faces to turn); li visi is the direct object 'the faces'.
segments.purgatorio-14-g14: per + infinitive (per dirmi) expresses purpose: 'in order to tell me.' The infinitive dire takes the enclitic object mi (to me).
segments.purgatorio-14-g15: supini is an adjective (plural) modifying visi: 'faces turned upward' (literally 'supine, on their backs'), not the grammatical 'supine.'
segments.purgatorio-14-g16: e 'and'; disse is passato remoto (3rd sg) of dire, 'he said'; l’uno = 'the one' (the first of the two spirits) — this introduces direct speech.
segments.purgatorio-14-g17: O anima is a vocative address 'O soul.' che introduces a relative clause describing anima. fitta here is archaic/poetic; it behaves like an adjective meaning 'fixed/settled' (i.e. 'still fixed in the body'); nel corpo ancora = 'still in the body.' inver’ is an archaic preposition/contraction for verso/toward (followed by lo ciel 'heaven'); ten is an archaic/truncated form whose exact morphological identity is irregular here (see note), and vai is 2nd-person singular present 'you go' or an imperative sense 'go.'
segments.purgatorio-14-g18: per carità is an appeal 'for charity's sake' (per + noun); ne is a clitic referring back to the speakers/us ('console us and tell us'), and consola and ditta are imperatives (or imperative-like present forms) addressing the soul: 'console (ne) and tell (ne).' onde here functions as 'whence/from where' (archaic usage), vieni is present indicative 'you come,' and chi se’ is 'who you are' (se’ is an archaic 2nd-person copula).
segments.purgatorio-14-g19: ché = 'for/because'; tu = 'you'; ne again is the clitic referring to the feelings of the speakers ('you make us'); fai is present 'you make' (tu fai), tanto maravigliar = 'so great an astonishment' (maravigliar = to astonish), de la tua grazia = 'at your grace/favor.'
segments.purgatorio-14-g20: quanto introduces a comparative/quantifier 'as much as' / 'how much'; vuol (from volere) here has the impersonal/venire a sense 'is fitting/requires' (i.e. 'as much as a thing requires'); cosa = 'a thing'; che non fu più mai = 'that never was before' (non + past fu 'was' + più mai 'ever/again' — a poetic way to say 'never').
segments.purgatorio-14-g21: "E io:" is the speaker's introduction. E is the simple coordinating conjunction "and"; io is the explicit subject pronoun "I" placed after the conjunction and followed by a colon to mark direct speech.
segments.purgatorio-14-g22: Per mezza Toscana is a fronted adverbial phrase meaning "through/over half of Tuscany" (per = through; mezza = "half"). Si spazia is a pronominal/intransitive use of spaziare (here "ranges/extends itself") with the clitic si; the subject un fiumicel (postverbal, a common poetic order) follows the verb. che nasce in Falterona is a relative clause (che = "which") modifying fiumicel; nasce is present indicative meaning "springs/arises," and in Falterona gives the source/place.
segments.purgatorio-14-g23: e introduces the coordinating clause. cento miglia di corso (here represented by miglia di corso) is the measure "a hundred miles of course/flow." Nol is the archaic contracted negative + clitic (non + lo = "not it/it not") placed before the verb, and sazia (present indicative of saziare) means "satisfies" or "sates." Together: "a hundred miles of its course do not satisfy it."
segments.purgatorio-14-g24: Di sovr' esso = "concerning/about that" (Di + sovra/abbr. sovr' used here). Rech' is an archaic/poetic form of a verb meaning "I bring" (recare), with the subject pronoun io placed after the verb (rech' io = "I bring"); questa persona is the direct object "this person."
segments.purgatorio-14-g25: dirvi is the infinitive dire with the enclitic indirect-object pronoun -vi attached, "to tell you." ch'i' sia contracts che + io ("that I") and sia is the present subjunctive of essere; the subjunctive is used in subordinate clauses of reported content or potential/uncertain statements introduced by an infinitive construction.
segments.purgatorio-14-g26: saria is an archaic conditional form (modern sarebbe) meaning "would be." It governs the following infinitive parlare: saria parlare = "it would be to speak"; indarno is an adverb "in vain." The whole phrase means "to tell you ... would be to speak in vain."
segments.purgatorio-14-g27: ché = "for/because" introduces the reason. 'l nome mio = "my name" (the article 'l = il is elided). ancor molto non suona uses suona (present indicative of suonare, literally "sounds") idiomatically: "is heard of / is known." ancor molto non suona = "is not yet much heard/known."
segments.purgatorio-14-g28: Se ben = "if indeed" or "if well." lo 'ntendimento tuo = "your understanding" (lo = article; 'ntendimento = intendimento/intendimento, archaic elision of initial vowel). accarno is an archaic/obscure verb form here meaning roughly "corresponds/agrees/answers to" (the sense is idiomatic: "if your understanding accords with your intellect"); con lo 'ntelletto = "with the intellect." Note several archaic elisions (’nt-) and the juxtaposition of two cognitive nouns.
segments.purgatorio-14-g29: allora = "then/thereupon." mi rispuose is passato remoto (rìspose) 3rd person singular of rispondere + indirect object mi = "answered me." quei che diceva pria = "he who was speaking earlier/that one who spoke before" (quei = that one; diceva = imperfect "was saying/speaking"; pria = previously).
segments.purgatorio-14-g30: «tu parli d’Arno». is direct speech: tu parli is present indicative "you speak" or "you mean," and d’ = di + elision "of"; Arno is the proper name (the river). The short reply identifies the river the speaker meant.
segments.purgatorio-14-g31: E l’altro disse lui: is a simple narrative line: E = and; l’altro = "the other" (the second of the two spirits); disse is passato remoto "said" and lui serves as the indirect-object pronoun "to him" (disse lui = "said to him"). Word order places the indirect object after the verb for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-14-g32: Perché = "why." nascose is passato remoto of nascondere "hid/concealed." questi is an archaic demonstrative/pronoun "this one/this man" (subject of nascose). il vocabol = "the word/name" (vocabol = older form of vocabol(o) 'word/name'); di quella riviera = "of that river/riverbank" (genitive relation). The clause asks why the man kept the river's name hidden.
segments.purgatorio-14-g33: pur com' om fa de l'orribili cose is a comparative/explanatory tag: pur (here "even/just") + com' (short for come = "as") + om (archaic for uomo, generic "one/man") + fa (Present of fare "does") + de (introducing topic "about/of") + l'orribili cose ("the horrible things"). Literally: "even as one does about horrible things."
segments.purgatorio-14-g56: 'Vassi' is an archaic/poetic 3rd‑person form built on va + si and is used impersonally: 'it goes' / 'it moves on.' 'Caggendo' is a present gerund of caggere (to fall, sink), used adverbially: together the phrase means 'it goes on by falling' or 'it keeps falling.'
segments.purgatorio-14-g57: This is the first half of a correlative comparison (quant'... tanto...): 'e' = and; 'quant'' (short for quanto) introduces 'how much / the more'; 'ella' here is neuter/impersonal 'it' (referring back to the pit/thing described); 'più' = 'more'; '’ngrossa' (poetic spelling of ingrossa) = 'grows in bulk, swells.' The phrase = 'and the more it swells'.
segments.purgatorio-14-g58: 'Tanto più' completes the correlative: 'the more ... the more.' 'Trova' is 3rd person singular present 'finds.' The clause that follows is inverted: the logical subject is 'la maladetta e sventurata fossa' (the accursed, ill‑fated pit) which comes after the verb for poetic effect. 'Di can farsi lupi' means 'that dogs make themselves/become wolves' — here farsi is reflexive in the sense 'to become' (poetic usage).
segments.purgatorio-14-g59: 'Discesa' is a past participle used absolutively (adverbial) — 'having descended.' 'Poi' = then/next. 'Per' = through; 'più' here = 'many' (several); 'pelaghi' = open seas/expanses of sea; 'cupi' = gloomy/dark — together: 'having descended further through many dark seas.'
segments.purgatorio-14-g60: 'Trova' = 'finds' (3rd person sing.). 'Le volpi' = the foxes (direct object); 'sì piene di froda' = 'so full of fraud/guile' (sì = so, piene = full, di froda = of deceit). The adjective phrase follows the noun as predicate complement.
segments.purgatorio-14-g61: The 'che' here continues/result clause: 'that they do not fear...' 'Non temono' is present indicative 3rd person plural 'they do not fear.' 'Ingegno' = cleverness/ingenuity (object of temono). The second 'che' introduces a subordinate relative clause with an archaic subjunctive form 'occùpi' (stress mark preserved): 'that might occupy/seize them,' i.e. 'that could get the better of them.'
segments.purgatorio-14-g62: 'Né lascerò di dir' = 'Nor will I stop saying' (lascerò = future 'I will leave/let/cease'; di + infinitive = to do something). 'Perch’' (poetic truncation of perché) here has a final/purpose sense 'so that'; 'altri' = other people/others; 'm’' = clitic me ('mi'); 'oda' is present subjunctive 3rd person ('may hear') used in a purpose clause expressing the desired outcome.
segments.purgatorio-14-g63: 'E buon sarà costui' = 'and this fellow will be good' (lit. 'and good will be this one') — a common inverted order in poetry where the predicate ('buon') precedes the future verb 'sarà' and the subject 'costui' follows.
segments.purgatorio-14-g64: The clause 's’ancor s’ammenta' = 'if he still remembers' where the first 's’' is the conditional 'se' (if) contracted, 'ancor' = still, and the second 's’' is the reflexive clitic attached to 'ammenta' (si‑ammenta = remembers). 'Di ciò che vero spirto mi disnoda' = 'of that which the true spirit unties for me' — 'disnoda' (present) = 'unravels/unties' (figurative: reveals or frees), and 'mi' is the indirect object 'to me.'
segments.purgatorio-14-g65: Io 'I'. veggio is a poetic/archaic verb (here 'I behold/see'); tuo nepote = 'your nephew.' che introduces a relative clause 'who becomes' (diventa = present 'becomes'); cacciator di quei lupi = 'hunter of those wolves.' in su la riva = 'on the bank' (in su = up/on), del fiero fiume = 'of the fierce/terrible river' (del = di + il).
segments.purgatorio-14-g66: e 'and'; tutti 'all' (plural) with li (here functioning as object pronoun 'them' or as the old form of the article), sgomenta is present 3rd singular of sgomentare 'frightens/dismays' — so 'and he frightens them all.'
segments.purgatorio-14-g158: Ma is the adversative conjunction 'but'. Voi prendete is present indicative, 2nd-person plural (or polite/plural 'you') of prendere, meaning 'you take'; the subject pronoun voi is explicit for force or address. L’esca is the direct object with the article elided (l’ = il + vowel), so the clause reads literally 'But you take the bait.'
segments.purgatorio-14-g159: Sì che introduces a result ('so that/so as to'), followed by the clause whose verb is tira (3rd person singular present of tirare). L’amo de l’antico avversaro is a genitive construction ('the hook of the ancient adversary') with de = 'of'. A sé vi tira places the reflexive phrase a sé ('to himself') before the clitic vi ('you') and the finite verb tira; vi is the clitic object 'you', so the whole clause means 'so that the (opponent's) hook draws you to himself.' Note that amo here is a noun ('hook'), not the verb 'amo'.
segments.purgatorio-14-g160: E però links back to the previous clause ('and therefore'). Poco val is a compact, somewhat literary inversion meaning 'is of little value/it avails little': poco modifies the impersonal/predicative verb val (from valere, 'to be worth' — here used in 3rd singular). Freno o richiamo are the items that 'avail little' (they are the logical subject/complements), 'a bridle or a call' — richiamo is a noun 'call/summons/recall'.
segments.purgatorio-14-g161: Chiamavi is the imperfect form of chiamare ('you called' / 'you were calling') with an older -vi ending (addressing 'you', singular or poetically used). ’l cielo is the direct object 'the heaven' (elided article). E ’ntorno vi si gira: 'e' = 'and'; '’ntorno' is an elided form of intorno 'around'; vi is the clitic meaning 'around you' (direct/indirect object depending on verb); si gira is the reflexive 3rd-person singular of girare ('turns itself/turns') so the phrase means 'and it turns around you.'
segments.purgatorio-14-g162: Mostrandovi is the gerund/present participle 'mostrando' with the clitic vi attached (showing you). Le sue bellezze: le = the (plural); sue = 'its/his/her' — here 'its' referring back to the heaven; bellezze = plural noun 'beauties'. Etterne is an archaic/spelling variant of eterne, 'eternal.' The whole participial phrase modifies the previous clause: 'showing you its eternal beauties.'
segments.purgatorio-14-g163: E = 'and'. L’occhio vostro = 'your eye' with the article l’ before occhio; pur = 'still' / 'yet' emphasizing continuity. A terra mira uses mira (3rd person singular present of mirare) with the preposition a: mira a terra = 'looks/gazes toward the earth.' Thus 'and your eye still gazes at the earth.'
segments.purgatorio-14-g164: Onde here functions as a connective 'wherefore/whence/so' (archaic usage) introducing the result. Vi is again the clitic 'you' (meaning 'upon you' / 'to you'); batte is 3rd-person singular of battere 'strikes/beats' (here 'you are struck'). Chi tutto discerne is a relative/indefinite phrase 'he who discerns all' (i.e. the one who sees/judges everything). The clause therefore means roughly 'wherefore you are struck by He who discerns all.'
segments.purgatorio-14-g95: E non pur = 'and not only' (non pur functions as 'not only' rather than a simple negation). 'Lo suo sangue' uses the archaic order with the masculine article lo before the possessive suo; 'sangue' here is used metaphorically (lineage/stock). 'È fatto brullo' is a passive/resultative: essere + past participle fatto + adjective brullo = 'has been made barren/laid waste.'
segments.purgatorio-14-g96: 'tra' = 'between/among'. The repeated elided article '’l' stands for il (the) before each place-name; Dante strings the coordinate places with e = 'and'. The sequence lists geographical bounds: the Po (river), the monte (hill/mountain), the marina (seashore), and the Reno (Rhine).
segments.purgatorio-14-g97: 'del ben richesto' = 'of the well-sought good/wealth' — 'ben' functions as a noun (goods/wealth) and 'richesto' is a past participle used adjectivally ('sought after/requested'). 'al vero e al trastullo' = 'for the true/necessitous (profit) and for pastime' — al = a + il (to/for the); 'vero' here denotes 'the real/rightful' or 'true need', and 'trastullo' means amusement/trifle.
segments.purgatorio-14-g98: Ché = 'for/because'. 'dentro a questi termini' = 'within these boundaries/limits' ('dentro a' is a common medieval/local idiom equivalent to dentro + (prep)). 'È ripieno di venenosi …' is the present-state statement 'is full of poisonous …' with ripieno as predicative adjective meaning 'filled/full.'
segments.purgatorio-14-g99: Sì che = 'so that' (introduces the consequence). 'tardi per coltivare' = 'too late to cultivate' — per + infinitive expresses purpose/ability here. 'omai' = 'by now/any longer', and 'verrebber' is the archaic conditional (they would; cf. modern verrebbero) in plural; 'meno' qualifies the result ('less' / 'to little effect').
segments.purgatorio-14-g100: Ov’ is the contracted form of 'ov'è' = 'where is'. Dante uses the singular verb è though two names follow; this is a rhetorical ellipsis (treating the pair as a single object of the inquiry) rather than strict modern agreement. 'l buon Lizio' = 'the good Lizio' (article + adjective + name).
segments.purgatorio-14-g101: This is an elliptical question: the verb 'ov'è' from the previous line is omitted and understood. The line simply lists the two names; 'Traversaro' functions as a surname (here worded like an apposition) and 'di Carpigna' uses the preposition di to indicate origin/family.
segments.purgatorio-14-g102: Oh = exclamation 'O'. 'Romagnuoli' is the plural ethnonym 'Romagnols' (people from Romagna). 'tornati' is the past participle of tornare used predicatively to mean 'become/turned (into)' here; the verb 'essere' is omitted, typical in an exclamatory address. 'in bastardi' = 'into bastards' — an insulting judgment, with 'bastardi' used as a reproach.
segments.purgatorio-14-g136: Noi sapavam is an archaic imperfect (modern sapevamo) meaning 'we knew'. che introduces a subordinate clause. quell’ anime care = 'those dear souls' (quell' + plural noun is a poetic contraction). ci sentivano andar: sentivano is imperfect of sentire 'to perceive/feel', ci is the direct object pronoun 'us', and andar is the bare infinitive used after a perception verb to indicate the action perceived ('perceived us going'). Word order puts the object pronoun before the verb, as usual.
segments.purgatorio-14-g137: però is an adverb meaning 'therefore/so' here, linking the previous knowledge to the consequence that follows.
segments.purgatorio-14-g138: tacendo is the gerund of tacere ('being silent'); it functions adverbially ('by/while being silent') and modifies the action described in the next clause.
segments.purgatorio-14-g139: facëan is an imperfect third-person plural (modern facevano) of fare used causatively 'they made/did'; the subject (they) is the souls mentioned earlier. noi is the object 'us' placed after the verb. del cammin confidare is an infinitival complement: confidare ('to trust/rely') takes del cammin ('in regard to the path/journey') as its complement — so 'they made us trust the path' (the diaeresis in facëan marks the pronounced vowel).
segments.purgatorio-14-g140: Poi 'then'. fummo fatti is a compound/resultative construction: fummo (past of essere) + fatti (past participle of fare) = 'we were made' / 'we became' (passive/result). soli 'alone' agrees with the implied subject (noi); procedendo is a gerund 'proceeding/going on' giving the temporal setting ('as we went on').
segments.purgatorio-14-g141: folgore 'lightning, flash' is the grammatical subject of parve (third-person singular of parere, here 'seemed'). quando introduces a temporal clause: l’aere fende — l'aere 'the air/sky' is subject and fende is a present/historical-present verb 'cleaves/splits' (picture of a lightning cleaving the air).
segments.purgatorio-14-g142: voce 'a voice' is head of the noun phrase; che is the relative pronoun 'that/which' introducing the clause giunse di contra dicendo. giunse is past of giungere 'arrived/came'; di contra is an adverbial phrase 'from/against the opposite side' (di + contra); dicendo is a gerund 'saying', indicating the content of the arriving voice.
segments.purgatorio-14-g143: This is the quoted utterance. Anciderammi is an older fused form (ancideran mi) meaning 'they/whoever will kill me' — a future/resultive sense; qualunque introduces an indefinite relative 'whoever/whichever/anyone who'; m’ is the enclitic object pronoun 'me'; apprende is present tense of apprendere meaning 'takes hold of/seizes' here. Together: 'Whoever seizes me will kill me.'
segments.purgatorio-14-g144: e 'and'. fuggì is perfect/past (third singular) 'it/the voice fled/vanished' — the subject is the voice. come introduces a simile 'like'; tuon (short for tuono) 'thunder'; che si dilegua is a relative clause: si dilegua = reflexive dileguarsi 'vanishes/dissipates'.
segments.purgatorio-14-g145: se 'if/when' (introducing the conditional/temporal clause). sùbito here is adverbial ('suddenly'); la nuvola 'the cloud' is subject, and scoscende is a verb meaning 'slopes/falls down' — 'when the cloud suddenly descends' explains the condition under which the thunder-like sound disappears.
segments.purgatorio-14-g146: Come introduces a temporal/similar clause 'as/when.' da lei l’udir nostro ebbe triegua = 'from her our hearing received respite' — l’udir nostro 'our hearing' is the subject, ebbe is passato remoto 'received', triegua = 'a short respite.'
segments.purgatorio-14-g147: ed ecco l’altra = 'and behold the other (female spirit)'; con sì gran fracasso = 'with so great a crash/uproar' (sì gran = so great/intense, fracasso = noise/crash).
segments.purgatorio-14-g148: che somigliò tonar che tosto segua: che = 'that/which'; somigliò = passato remoto 'it resembled/seemed like'; tonar = 'thundering' (infinitive used as noun-like); che tosto segua = 'which would quickly follow' (tosto = soon/quickly, segua = present subjunctive/optative 'follow').
segments.purgatorio-14-g149: Direct quotation: Io sono Aglauro = 'I am Aglauros'; che divenni sasso = relative clause 'who became stone' (divenni = past tense 'became', sasso = 'a stone').
segments.purgatorio-14-g151: per ristrignermi al poeta: per + infinitive expresses purpose/reason 'to/so as to ristrignermi (retract or draw myself in/tighten myself),' where ristrignermi is reflexive 'to contract myself' and al poeta = 'to the poet' (i.e. toward/with regard to the poet).
segments.purgatorio-14-g152: in destro feci: in destro = 'to the right' (destro = right-hand side), feci is passato remoto 1st singular 'I made/did' — 'I made (a movement) to the right.'
segments.purgatorio-14-g153: e non innanzi = 'and not forward/in front' (innanzi = in front/before). The negative non negates innanzi, specifying the direction of the step.
segments.purgatorio-14-g154: il passo = 'the step/passage' (direct object of feci): 'I made the step to the right, not forward.'
segments.purgatorio-14-g155: Già is a fronted adverb meaning 'already' (emphasising time). Era is the imperfect/past tense of essere ('was'). L’ is the elided article before a vowel; aura here (archaic/poetic) means 'air' or 'breeze' rather than the unrelated lemma gloss given, and queta is the adjective 'quiet' agreeing with aura; d’ogne parte is a prepositional phrase 'from/on every side' modifying where the air was quiet.
segments.purgatorio-14-g156: Ed is the form of e 'and' used before a vowel for euphony. El is an archaic subject pronoun meaning 'he' (modern Italian sarebbe lui). Mi is the indirect object 'to me'. Disse is passato remoto of dire ('said'), and the whole short clause introduces direct speech.
segments.purgatorio-14-g157: Quel is the demonstrative 'that'; fu is passato remoto of essere ('was'). ’l is the elided definite article 'the' (before duro). Duro is the adjective 'hard'; camo is an archaic noun here (the provided senses do not match the context) meaning something like 'burden/trouble/affliction'. Che is the relative 'which/that'. Dovria is an archaic modal form ('ought to' / 'should') and note the slightly inverted order here (verb before subject: dovria l’uom tener rather than the modern l’uom dovria tener). Tener is the infinitive 'to keep' and dentro + a sua meta ('within/inside his goal/destination') states the limit or boundary within which man should keep that burden.
segments.purgatorio-14-g67: Vende is 3rd-person singular present (the narrative/historic present) meaning "he/she/it sells." «la carne loro» is the direct object: la carne (feminine singular) "the flesh" with the post‑nominal possessive loro "their." «essendo viva» is a present gerund/participle construction "being alive" that modifies la carne (viva agrees in gender with carne); it provides the circumstance in which the selling takes place.
segments.purgatorio-14-g68: poscia is an adverb "afterwards/then." li ancide is a verbal clause: ancide (3rd‑person singular of the poetic verb ancidere) "kills/slays," with the clitic li as its direct object pronoun ("them"). come introduces a simile "like/as," and antica belva is the simile's noun phrase "an old/ancient beast," with antica agreeing with belva.
segments.purgatorio-14-g69: Here priva is a 3rd‑person singular present of privare (feminine, agreeing with the implied subject 'la belva' from the previous clause): "(she) deprives." Molti is the (direct) object "many." The phrases di vita and sé di pregio state what they are deprived of: "of life and of themselves of worth/esteem" (sé = themselves; di pregio = 'of worth/value').
segments.purgatorio-14-g70: Sanguinoso is an adjective (masculine singular) "bloodstained/bloody" modifying the implied subject (the beast). esce is 3rd‑person singular present "comes out/emerges." de is the archaic form of da "from," and la trista selva is the noun phrase "the wretched/sad forest," with trista agreeing with selva.
segments.purgatorio-14-g71: lasciala is the imperative or jussive sense of lasciare with the enclitic pronoun -la: "leave it/her." tal (short for tale) means "such/so." The clause is a direct command addressed to an interlocutor: "leave it like that."
segments.purgatorio-14-g72: che introduces a result/subordinate clause "so that/that." di qui a mille anni means "from here / for a thousand years" (i.e. "for a thousand years hence"). ne lo stato primaio: ne (= into/in) + lo stato primaio "the former state/condition" (primaio = former/earlier). non si rinselva is a negated reflexive verb form: si (reflexive) + rinselva (3rd‑person singular of rinselvare, "become wooded again / reforest") → "it will not/does not rewood itself."
segments.purgatorio-14-g73: Com’ is the contracted form of come "as/just as." a l’annunzio "at the announcement" (a + l' = at the). di dogliosi danni "of sorrowful losses" qualifies annunzio. si turba is a reflexive/passive‑like construction (si + turb a) meaning "is troubled / becomes disturbed." il viso di colui ch’ascolta = "the face of him who listens," where colui (that one) + ch’ (che, who) + ascolta (3rd‑person singular present) form the relative clause.
segments.purgatorio-14-g74: da qual che parte is an idiomatic expression "from whichever side/direction." il periglio is the subject "the danger." l’assanni is the verb phrase where the clitic l’ refers back to colui ("him") and assanni is a poetic verb meaning "assails/afflicts"; it denotes the action of the danger striking from a direction. (The verb form is archaic/poetic.)
segments.purgatorio-14-g75: così "thus/so." vid’ is the contracted archaic past tense (vidi) "I saw" (the final -i dropped for the verse), followed by the subject pronoun io (optional emphasis). l’altr’ anima "the other soul" (altr' = altro elided before a vowel).
segments.purgatorio-14-g76: che is a relative pronoun "who/which." volta here functions as a participial/adjectival notion "turned" (volta agrees with anima, feminine) — i.e. having turned round. stava is the imperfect of stare "was standing/was positioned" and with a + udir (a udir = "to hear") forms the periphrasis "was standing to hear" = "was turned/listening to hear."
segments.purgatorio-14-g77: turbarsi and farsi are infinitives with reflexive sense: turbarsi "to become disturbed" and farsi trista "to make/become oneself sad." The two infinitives share the same subject (the soul) and describe successive changes of state.
segments.purgatorio-14-g78: poi ch’ = poi che, "after/when." ebbe is the passato remoto (3rd‑person singular) of avere "had." la parola a sé raccolta is a compact phrase: la parola "the word/speech" + a sé "to herself" + raccolta (past participle) "gathered" — overall meaning "after she had gathered her words to herself" (i.e. after collecting/withdrawing her speech).
segments.purgatorio-14-g79: Lo dir de l’una e de l’altra la vista is a compact, slightly inverted phrase: 'the sight (la vista) of the one and of the other' (lo dir de = 'the saying/telling of' — lo dir here nominalizes the action). mi fer voglioso di saper lor nomi: fer (archaic past) 'made' (caused), mi = 'me', voglioso = 'desirous/eager', di saper = 'to know', lor nomi = 'their names.'
segments.purgatorio-14-g80: e 'and'; dimanda here is a noun or verbal noun 'a question/ask' and fei is the first-person past 'I made' (feci/fei) — 'and I made inquiry about them'; ne = clitic 'about them' or 'of them'; con prieghi mista = 'with prayers/entreaties mingled' (mista agrees with prieghi).
segments.purgatorio-14-g81: per che = 'wherefore/thereupon/for which reason'; lo spirto = 'the spirit'; che di pria parlòmi = relative clause 'who spoke to me before' (pria = 'earlier/previously', parlòmi = 'spoke to me'); ricominciò = passato remoto 'began again/resumed.'
segments.purgatorio-14-g82: Direct speech. Tu 'you'; vuo’ (from volere) is 2nd-person present 'you want.' ch’io mi deduca is a subordinate clause introduced by che with ch’io = 'that I,' mi deduca is present subjunctive or imperative-like form of dedurre/deducere in the sense 'that I lead/draw out' (reflexive mi indicates 'I put myself to the action'); nel fare a te = 'in doing toward you' (nel + infinitive = 'in the act of doing'); ciò che tu far non vuo’mi = 'that which you do not want me to do' (tu far = 'you do', non vuo’mi = 'you do not want me [to].').
segments.purgatorio-14-g34: This segment is punctuation only: a single period marking the end of a line/sentence. There are no lexical items to analyze.
segments.purgatorio-14-g35: E ('and') is the coordinating conjunction joining this clause to the previous discourse. l’ombra 'the shade' uses the elided article + noun. che introduces a relative clause modifying l’ombra. di ciò is a prepositional phrase 'about this/of this.' domandata era is a passive periphrasis in the imperfect (era + past participle domandata), literally 'was asked' or 'had been asked,' indicating a past state resulting from an earlier question.
segments.purgatorio-14-g36: si sdebitò così: si is the reflexive/mediopassive marker here and sdebitò is passato remoto (3rd sg) of sdebitare meaning 'repaid' or, idiomatically, 'made answer/returned the favor.' così 'thus' introduces the reported reply that immediately follows.
segments.purgatorio-14-g37: Non so is a simple present indicative clause meaning 'I do not know.' Non negates the verb so (1st sg present of sapere). The line begins direct speech with an unelaborated, plain statement of ignorance.
segments.purgatorio-14-g38: ma introduces a contrast ('but'). degno ben è is an inverted copular phrase: ben (emphatic 'indeed') + è (present of essere) + degno ('fitting/appropriate'), literally 'but indeed it is fitting.' che introduces a subordinate clause with a subjunctive (see pèra) expressing what is fitting. ’l nome di tal valle is the logical subject of the subordinate clause. pèra is here a verb meaning 'perish/be lost' in an archaic/poetic form (not the noun 'pear'); because the provided lemma list lacks this verbal sense, it is marked -1.
segments.purgatorio-14-g39: ché is a conjunction meaning 'for' or 'because' that introduces the explanatory clause. dal principio suo is a prepositional phrase 'from its beginning' (dal = da + il), with suo = 'its' agreeing to principio.
segments.purgatorio-14-g40: ov’ (ov’) is an elided form of ove/ove 'where.' è sì pregno 'is so full' uses present indicative (è) with sì pregno ('so full/filled with'). l’alpestro monte is the noun phrase 'the rugged mountain' (alpestro = rough, alpine). ond’ è tronco Peloro is a relative/qualifying phrase using ond' (whence/from which) + è (is) + tronco Peloro: 'which is the trunk (or headland) of Peloro' — tronco here means 'trunk/stump/head' (sense 'trunk' rather than 'cut off').
segments.purgatorio-14-g41: che is the relative pronoun 'which.' ’n is an elided form of in (here printed as n), introducing the locative expression 'in pochi luoghi' ('in few places'); because the listed sense is the letter 'n' rather than the preposition, the correct lexical sense is not available and is marked -1. passa is present indicative 'passes/crosses,' oltra (older form of oltre) means 'beyond,' and quel segno 'that mark' completes the locative idea.
segments.purgatorio-14-g42: infin là ’ve = 'as far as where' (infin là + ’ve = ove/where). si rende is a reflexive/passive use of rendere; here it means 'is/are returned' or 'yields/turns back' used impersonally; per ristoro 'for refreshment/relief' specifies purpose. di quel che ’l ciel de la marina asciuga is a genitive clause 'of that which the sky drains/draws from the sea' (asciuga = 'dries/takes up'), describing the thing yielded as refreshment.
segments.purgatorio-14-g43: ond’ ('whence') links to the previous clause. hanno i fiumi is present indicative 'the rivers have/possess.' ciò che va con loro is a relative clause 'that which goes with them' (ciò che = 'what/that which'; va = 'goes'), indicating what the rivers receive or carry with them.
segments.purgatorio-14-g44: vertù ('virtue' or natural good quality) is the subject. così 'thus' ties back to the foregoing causes. per nimica si fuga uses per + adjective/noun nimica ('as an enemy/hostile') with reflexive si + verb fuga (from fugare) meaning 'flees' or 'is put to flight' — idiomatically 'virtue flees as if it were an enemy.' da tutti 'from everyone' and come biscia 'like a snake' complete the simile: virtue avoids people like a serpent avoiding them.
segments.purgatorio-14-g45: o per sventura 'or by the misfortune' uses sventura (a noun) with the preposition per to offer an alternative cause. del luogo ('of the place') specifies that the misfortune belongs to the location itself.
segments.purgatorio-14-g46: o per mal uso 'or by bad usage/practice' gives another causal alternative; mal is an archaic form meaning 'bad/ill' modifying uso ('use/custom'). che li fruga is a relative clause: che = 'which/that,' li = 'them' (direct object pronoun), and fruga (present indicative of frugare) means 'rummages through' or 'plunders/consumes them' — i.e., bad customs that prey on or erode them.
segments.purgatorio-14-g47: ond’ hanno sì mutata lor natura is a result clause headed by ond' ('whence/therefore'): hanno mutata (present perfect) = 'have changed' with mutata as the past participle agreeing in gender/number with the direct object; lor natura = 'their nature.' The order is slightly inverted for emphasis: the verb phrase precedes the explicit subject li abitator de la misera valle ('the inhabitants of the miserable valley').
segments.purgatorio-14-g48: che par che introduces a subordinate clause of appearance: par (present 'seems') + che triggers the subjunctive. Circe li avesse in pastura uses avesse (imperfect subjunctive of avere) because par che often takes the subjunctive in Dante; li = 'them' (direct object), and in pastura = 'in pasture' (i.e., as if Circe had turned them into beasts for her pasture). The sequence expresses a comparison: it seems as if Circe had them for pasture.
segments.purgatorio-14-g49: Tra is the preposition 'among/amongst.' brutti porci is an adjective + noun in the plural meaning 'filthy/ugly pigs.' The phrase sets the scene/location in which what follows happens.
segments.purgatorio-14-g50: più degni di galle che d’altro cibo is a comparative construction: più degni 'more deserving/fit' di galle 'of gall/bile' (galle here = bile, i.e. something fit to give bile rather than food). che d’altro cibo 'than of other food' completes the comparison. fatto in uman uso is a participial phrase 'made for human use' (fatto = made/fit, in uman uso = for human consumption/custom).
segments.purgatorio-14-g51: dirizza prima il suo povero calle: dirizza (here a verb, 3rd sg present) means 'directs/sets' (note: the provided lemma list treats dirizzo as a noun, so the verbal sense is not listed and is marked -1). prima 'first' indicates sequence; il suo povero calle = 'its/his poor path/way' (calle = lane/path). The verb governs the noun phrase as its direct object.
segments.purgatorio-14-g52: Botoli (plural noun) denotes packs of young dogs/hounds; trova is 3rd sg present of trovare 'finds/encounters,' and poi 'then' indicates sequence. The subject implied by trova is the same entity that directed the 'calle' in the previous line.
segments.purgatorio-14-g53: venendo giuso is a present gerund/participial phrase: venendo (gerund of venire) = 'coming,' giuso = 'down' (poetic variant of giù). The phrase modifies the finding/encounter, describing the motion of the packs.
segments.purgatorio-14-g54: ringhiosi più che non chiede lor possa is a comparative formula: ringhiosi 'snarling' (adjective describing the dogs); più che non introduces a limitative comparison 'more than' followed by chiede lor possa. chiede here is used idiomatically with non + possa (subjunctive) to mean 'more than their power/ability allows' — literally 'more than (what) their power asks of them' i.e. 'more than they can sustain.' lor is the clitic 'to them' or 'their' depending on idiom, and possa is present subjunctive of potere used after the negative comparative.
segments.purgatorio-14-g55: e da lor disdegnosa torce il muso: e 'and'; da lor 'from them' (da + clitic/old-form lor). disdegnosa 'disdainful' is an adjectival modifier agreeing with the implicit subject (feminine in Dante's idiom when referring to a beast or the path). torce (3rd sg present of torcere) = 'twists/turns' and il muso 'the snout/face' is the direct object: 'turns its/one's disdainful face away from them.'
segments.purgatorio-14-g83: Ma = 'but' introduces a contrast. Da che here is a causal, literary 'since' (literally 'from that') and introduces the subordinate clause. Dio in te vuol che traluca = 'God wants that ... shine through in you': vuol (3rd-singular of volere) governs a subordinate clause introduced by che; traluca is the verb 'tralucere' (to shine through/appear) and here functions as the finite verb of that subordinate clause (a subjunctive/indicative form that in this poetic register reads as a present potential). In te ('in you') marks the location/recipient of the shining; tanto sua grazia places the intensity (tanto modifies grazia) and posta after the verb as object/predicate.
segments.purgatorio-14-g84: Non ti sarò scarso = 'I will not be scant/sparing to you.' Non negates the sentence; ti is the clitic object (dative-like 'toward you') placed before the finite verb; sarò is the future of essere ('I will be'); scarso is an adjective here meaning 'sparing / stingy' (so 'non sarò scarso' = 'I will not be stingy').
segments.purgatorio-14-g85: Però = 'therefore / so' introduces the consequence. Sappi is the 2nd-person singular imperative of sapere ('know! / be sure to know'); ch’ = che 'that' introduces the content of the imperative; io fui = 'I was' uses fui (past, perfect / passato remoto) to state a past identity; Guido del Duca is a proper name (Guido with the epithet/locative 'del Duca').
segments.purgatorio-14-g86: Fu = 'was' (past tense, literary past/preterite). Il sangue mio = 'my blood' is the grammatical subject; d’invidia = 'from/with envy' marks the cause or quality; sì rïarso = 'so parched' (sì intensifies rïarso, a past participle/adjective meaning withered/parched; the ï indicates a diaeresis marking pronunciation).
segments.purgatorio-14-g87: Che introduces the result/consequence clause ('so... that'). Se introduces a hypothetical condition ('if'). Veduto avesse is an inverted pluperfect subjunctive (participle veduto before auxiliary avesse) meaning 'had seen'; this inversion is a poetic word-order. Uom is the archaic form of uomo ('man'/'anyone'); farsi lieto = reflexive farsi + adjective 'lieto' means 'to make oneself/become joyful' (farsi expresses change of state).
segments.purgatorio-14-g88: Visto here is the past participle of vedere placed before the conditional auxiliary avresti (poetic/inverted sequence) to form 'you would have seen.' M’ (me) is the clitic direct object attached before the auxiliary; avresti is 2nd-person singular conditional of avere used with visto; di livore sparso = 'sprinkled/strewn with livore' (livore = spite/bitterness, sparso = past participle 'spread' used adjectivally).
segments.purgatorio-14-g89: Di mia semente = 'from my seed' (semente here can mean literal seed but often 'offspring' metaphorically). Cotal is an archaic form of cotale 'such'; paglia 'straw' is a metaphor for something worthless; mieto is 1st-person present ('I reap/harvest') from mietere — a simple, emphatic present describing what he gathers from his seed.
segments.purgatorio-14-g90: O is the vocative interjection 'O' (addressing); gente umana is a vocative phrase 'human people / humankind' — an apostrophe to humanity. The comma marks the direct address.
segments.purgatorio-14-g91: Perché = 'why' (interrogative). Poni is 2nd-person present of porre ('you place/put'); 'l core = 'the heart' (direct object). Là 'v' è = 'there where is' (’v’ is a poetic contraction for 'ove'/'vi' meaning 'where'); mestier = 'need' or 'necessity'; di consorte divieto = a genitive-like phrase 'of consort (spouse) prohibition' — together: 'where there is need, yet a spouse is prohibited' or more naturally 'where companionship/marriage is forbidden.' Note the archaic order and contractions typical of Dante.
segments.purgatorio-14-g92: Questi = 'this one' (demonstrative/identifying pronoun) used as subject; è = 'is'; Rinier is the proper name. Simple copular identification.
segments.purgatorio-14-g93: Questi è 'l pregio e l’onore = 'this one is the pride and the honor' — pregio and onore are predicates linked by e. De la casa da Calboli = 'of the house/family of Calboli' (de/la = of the; casa here = family/lineage; da Calboli names the family origin). The clause praises his status within the family.
segments.purgatorio-14-g94: Ove = 'where'; nullo fatto = 'no deed' (nullo modifying fatto); s’è reda = reflexive/passive 'si è reda' with reda meaning 'heir/descendant' — the sense is 'no deed has since become an heir' (i.e. produced an heir) — poi = 'afterwards/since'; del suo valore = 'to/of his worth' (genitive complement describing what the heir would inherit). The line laments that no subsequent deed produced an heir worthy of his valor.
segments.purgatorio-14-g118: "Ben fa" is an idiom meaning "does well" or "is right to"; "fa" is the 3rd-person singular present of fare, and the subject (Bagnacaval) follows the verb in inverted order, a common poetic word order.
segments.purgatorio-14-g119: "che" introduces a relative clause "who/that"; "non" negates; "rifiglia" is an archaic 3rd-person singular present of rifigliare, here meaning roughly "to have/engender offspring again" (an older word for siring/producing children or issue).
segments.purgatorio-14-g120: "e mal fa" = "and does badly/acts ill"; "mal" is an adverb modifying "fa"; the proper name "Castrocaro" is the subject following the verb in the same inverted, emphatic order.
segments.purgatorio-14-g121: The coordinating "e" = "and" links another clause; "peggio" is a comparative adverb/predicative meaning "worse" (it modifies the implied verb "fa"); "Conio" is the proper name (here a town/family) and the subject.
segments.purgatorio-14-g122: "che" introduces a relative clause "who/that"; "di" here introduces the infinitive construction "di figliar" (literally "in begetting" / "by fathering"); "figliar" is the infinitive "to beget/bring forth"; "tai" (archaic "tali") = "such"; "conti" (here likely "counts", i.e. nobles or offspring of countly rank) is the direct object of "figliar"; "più" = "more/any longer"; "s’" = reflexive clitic "si" used here in "s’impiglia"; "impiglia" (3rd sing.) from impigliare = "to entangle/get snagged" — so the clause says that by fathering such counts he only gets more entangled.
segments.purgatorio-14-g123: "Ben faranno" is future tense, 3rd-person plural of fare: "they will do well"; the subject "i Pagan" (the Pagans) follows the verb (inverted order for emphasis); "Ben" is the adverb "well/indeed."
segments.purgatorio-14-g124: "da che" = "since/now that"; "’l demonio" = "the demon/devil"; "girà" is an archaic form of the verb "girare" used here with perfective meaning "has turned/has gone"; the phrase as a whole means "since the demon has gone away from them" (the sense of motion away is encoded in the older idiom underlying the line).
segments.purgatorio-14-g125: "ma non però che" is a negating concessive pattern: "but not so that / it is not the case that"; "puro" here functions as "pure/clear" (used adverbially/qualifying the following noun), "già mai" is an emphatic time expression "ever/at any time"; "rimagna" is an archaic subjunctive/present form of rimanere used after the negating conjunction (i.e. "remains" in the sense of "there remains"); "d’essi testimonio" = "any testimony about/of them."
segments.purgatorio-14-g126: "O" is the vocative interjection addressing someone; "Ugolin" is the proper name; "de’ Fantolin" is a family or origin tag ("of the Fantolini"); in poetry this marks lineage or affiliation.
segments.purgatorio-14-g127: Inverted order: the predicate adjective "sicuro" (= "secure/safe") comes before the verb "è" (3rd-singular of essere), followed by the noun phrase "’l nome tuo" (the name of yours = your name). The phrase states that his reputation/name is safe.
segments.purgatorio-14-g128: "da che" = "since/now that"; "più non s’aspetta" = "one no longer expects" ("s’" = impersonal/reflexive "si" giving an impersonal passive sense; "aspetta" = "expects/looks for"); "chi far lo possa" = a subordinate clause whose "chi" = "who/whoever" and "possa" is present subjunctive of potere ("who could/might be able to do it"). Together: since no one any longer is expected who could do it.
segments.purgatorio-14-g129: "Tralignando" is a gerund (present participle/gerundive) from tralignare meaning "deviating/straying" and indicates the manner in which someone might act — "by deviating" or "in straying/misleading fashion."
segments.purgatorio-14-g130: "Scuro" is an adjective meaning "dark/obscure"; placed at the end it sums up the effect: someone who, by deviating, could make it obscure (i.e. make the name dark/uncertain). The adjective is masculine singular, agreeing implicitly with the thing made obscure (the name).
segments.purgatorio-14-g131: "Ma va via" is an imperative/command idiom meaning "But go away"; in colloquial and poetic Italian "va" (3rd sing.) is often used for the 2nd-sing. imperative (cf. "va' via"). "Ma" = "but" introduces a turn in speech.
segments.purgatorio-14-g132: "Tosco" is a vocative: the speaker addresses someone as "Tuscan" (an ethnic/locational epithet) — short, direct address.
segments.purgatorio-14-g133: "omai" (archaic "omaie") = "now/any longer/by now" and here reinforces immediacy ("go away now").
segments.purgatorio-14-g134: "ch’" = "because/for"; "or" = "now"; "mi" = indirect object "to me"; "diletta" is a present-form meaning "delights/measures pleasing to me" (literally "it pleases me"); "troppo di pianger più che di parlare" = "too much in weeping more than in speaking" — "di + infinitive" expresses the activity enjoyed/undertaken, and "più che" is a comparison "more than."
segments.purgatorio-14-g135: "sì" = "thus/so" introducing the result; "m’ ha" = "has me" (literally "has to me"), where "ha" (3rd-sing. avere) functions causatively — "our reason has constrained my mind"; "nostra ragion" is the subject causing the state, and "la mente stretta" is the object-complement phrase "the mind [made] tight/strained." Word order is inverted for emphasis: object and its adjective follow the verb.
segments.purgatorio-14-g103: This is a single rhetorical question. 'Quando' is the interrogative adverb 'when' introducing the question; 'in Bologna' is a locative prepositional phrase placed after the question word; 'un Fabbro' is an indefinite noun phrase ('a smith'). The verb phrase 'si ralligna' is reflexive (si) with 'ralligna' in the present indicative, 3rd person singular — an archaic/poetic form here meaning something like 'becomes sulky/reluctant' and it closes the clause.
segments.purgatorio-14-g104: 'quando' again introduces a parallel rhetorical question; 'in Faenza' is the place phrase; 'un Bernardin di Fosco' is an indefinite noun plus a patronymic ('Bernardin, son/of Fosco'), with 'di' marking relation/origin. Word order is normal for an Italian NP following a temporal/place adverb.
segments.purgatorio-14-g105: 'verga gentil di picciola gramigna' is a noun phrase commenting on Bernardin: 'verga' (here a noun meaning 'stock/line' or similar) followed by the adjective 'gentil' (noble/highborn) and a genitive/complement 'di picciola gramigna' ('of small/poor stock'). Adjective/noun order reflects poetic style; the whole phrase completes the question begun by 'quando'.
segments.purgatorio-14-g106: 'Non ti maravigliar' is a negative imperative/ironical admonition meaning 'do not be surprised' (non + verb directed at 'ti' = you). The following 's’io piango' is a contracted conditional/ concessive clause ('se io' → 'if I' / 'that I'), where 'piango' is the 1st person present indicative 'I weep' and explains the reason for the preceding prohibition to be surprised.
segments.purgatorio-14-g107: A simple vocative: 'Tosco,' addresses the addressee as 'Tuscan.' The word stands alone and is set off by a comma.
segments.purgatorio-14-g108: 'quando rimembro' is a temporal subordinate phrase meaning 'when I remember'; 'quando' is the adverb 'when' and 'rimembro' is the present indicative 1st person 'I remember.' It links the opening clause to the memories that follow.
segments.purgatorio-14-g109: 'con Guido da Prata' is a prepositional phrase of accompaniment: 'con' = 'with', 'Guido' a proper name, and 'da Prata' a toponymic/identifying phrase ('of/ from Prata') formed with 'da' marking origin.
segments.purgatorio-14-g110: 'Ugolin d’Azzo' is a patronymic name construction ('Ugolin, son/of Azzo'); 'che' is the relative pronoun 'who/that' introducing a relative clause; 'vivette' is an archaic past-tense form ('lived') in the relative clause; 'nosco' here links the clause to the speaker's group (roughly 'among us'/'with us') and is dialectal/poetic.
segments.purgatorio-14-g111: 'Federigo Tignoso e sua brigata' is another coordinated NP: a personal name plus an epithet/nickname 'Tignoso', then 'e' connecting 'his brigata' — 'sua' is the possessive adjective 'his' and 'brigata' means 'band' or 'retinue'. Word order is normal: possessor adjective before the noun.
segments.purgatorio-14-g112: 'la casa Traversara e li Anastagi' lists two family/house names introduced by definite articles; 'la casa' here functions as 'the house/lineage of Traversara.' The parenthetical '(e l’una gente e l’altra è diretata)' uses the correlative 'l'una... l'altra' ('one... the other') and the verb 'è' (3rd person sing. present) plus the participial adjective 'diretata' agreeing with 'gente' (feminine) in this archaic/poetic form.
segments.purgatorio-14-g113: 'le donne e ’ cavalier' is a simple coordinated noun phrase 'the women and the knights.' The apostrophe before 'cavalier' reflects older orthography; articles and nouns agree in number/gender as typical.
segments.purgatorio-14-g114: 'li affanni e li agi' ('the troubles and the comforts') is the head of a relative clause introduced by 'che' ('which/that'). 'Ne' is a pronominal particle referring back to something in the context ('of/about them' or 'in them'), and '’nvogliava' (nvogliava) is an imperfect tense (3rd sing.) meaning 'aroused desire' or 'made one long for'; its objects are 'amore e cortesia.' The clause closes with a locative relative 'là dove' ('there where') and a passive/resultative construction 'i cuor son fatti' ('the hearts are made/become') followed by 'sì malvagi' ('so wicked'), with 'son' = present of 'essere' and 'fatti' a past participle used predicatively.
segments.purgatorio-14-g115: A vocative interjection: 'O' is the exclamation 'O/Oh' used to address 'Bretinoro' directly; the name stands in the vocative case position.
segments.purgatorio-14-g116: 'ché non fuggi via' is an interrogative clause meaning 'why do you not flee away?' Here 'ché' acts as a question particle ('why'), 'non' is the negation, and 'fuggi' is the 2nd-person present form (rhetorical present) of 'fuggire' — the clause asks for the reason the addressee does not depart; 'via' functions adverbially ('away').
segments.purgatorio-14-g117: 'poi che' here introduces a causal/explanatory clause ('since/now that'). The sequence 'gita se n’è la tua famiglia' is an archaic word order for 'your family has gone away' (with 'se n’è' = 'has of it/away' and a past form 'gita' indicating departure). 'E molta gente' continues ('and many people'), and 'per non esser ria' is a purpose/explanatory infinitive phrase ('so as not to be wicked' / 'to avoid being called wicked'), with 'esser' the infinitive 'to be' in archaic form.

sentences.purgatorio-14-s1: Who is this one who circles our mountain before death has given him flight, and opens his eyes at his pleasure and covers them?
sentences.purgatorio-14-s3: “I do not know who he is, but I know that he is not alone; you ask him yourself — you who are nearer to him — and gently, so that he may speak; go up to him.”
sentences.purgatorio-14-s4: Thus two spirits, each bent toward the other, were talking about me there on the right hand; then they turned their faces upward to speak to me, and one said: “O soul that still dwells in the body and yet turnst thy face toward heaven, go; for charity console us and tell us whence you come and who you are; for you make us marvel so much at your grace as may befit a thing that never was before.”
sentences.purgatorio-14-s5: And I said: "A little stream ranges across half of Tuscany, springing from Falterona, and a hundred miles of its course do not satisfy it."
sentences.purgatorio-14-s6: Concerning that I bring this person: to tell you who I am would be to speak in vain, for my name is not yet much known."
sentences.purgatorio-14-s7: "If well your understanding corresponds with your intellect," then he who was speaking before answered me, "you mean the Arno."
sentences.purgatorio-14-s8: And the other said to him: "Why did this one hide the name of that riverbank, even as one does about horrible things?"
sentences.purgatorio-14-s13: It goes on falling; and the more it swells, the more the accursed and ill‑fated pit finds dogs turning into wolves.
sentences.purgatorio-14-s14: Then, after descending through many dark expanses of sea, he finds the foxes so full of guile that they fear no cunning that might seize them.
sentences.purgatorio-14-s15: Nor will I cease to say it so that others may hear me; and he will be a good man, if he still remembers that which the true spirit unties for me.
sentences.purgatorio-14-s16: I see your nephew who becomes a hunter of those wolves on the bank of the fierce river, and he frightens them all.
sentences.purgatorio-14-s41: But you take the bait, so that the ancient adversary's hook draws you to himself; and therefore a bridle or a call is of little use.
sentences.purgatorio-14-s42: You called on heaven, and it turns around you, showing you its eternal beauties; and yet your eye still gazes at the earth; hence you are struck by He who discerns all.
sentences.purgatorio-14-s25: And not only has its blood (lineage/stock) been made barren between the Po and the hill and the sea and the Reno, of the goods sought both for true need and for pastime; for within these bounds it is full of poisonous brambles, so that it would now be too late for them to cultivate (it) with any success.
sentences.purgatorio-14-s26: Where is the good Lizio and Arrigo Mainardi?
sentences.purgatorio-14-s27: Pier Traversaro and Guido of Carpigna?
sentences.purgatorio-14-s37: We knew that those dear souls perceived us walking; therefore, keeping silent, they made us trust in the path.
sentences.purgatorio-14-s38: Then, as we proceeded, we were left alone; a flash of lightning seemed as the air split, and a voice came from the opposite side saying, 'Whoever seizes me will kill me'; and it fled like thunder that vanishes when a cloud suddenly sinks.
sentences.purgatorio-14-s39: When our hearing of her had respite, behold the other with so great a crash that it resembled thunder at once following: “I am Aglauros who became a stone”; and then, to keep myself tight to the poet, I made a step to the right, not forward.
sentences.purgatorio-14-s40: The air on every side was already quiet; and he said to me: "That was the hard burden which a man ought to keep within his goal."
sentences.purgatorio-14-s17: He sells their flesh while it is still alive; afterwards he slays them like an old beast; many are deprived of life and of their own worth.
sentences.purgatorio-14-s18: Bloodstained it comes out of the wretched wood; leave it so, that for a thousand years from now it will not return to its former wooded state.
sentences.purgatorio-14-s19: Just as, at the announcement of sorrowful losses, the face of him who listens is troubled — from whatever direction the danger assails him — so did I see the other soul, who was turned to hear, grow disturbed and become sad, after she had gathered her words to herself.
sentences.purgatorio-14-s20: The sight of the one and the other made me eager to learn their names, and I asked about them with entreaties mingled; wherefore the spirit who had spoken to me before began again: “You want me to lead myself into doing for you what you yourself do not want me to do.”
sentences.purgatorio-14-s10: And the shade that had been asked about this answered thus: "I do not know; but it is indeed fitting that the name of such a valley perish; for from its beginning, where the rugged mountain is so full, the truncated Peloro — which in few places passes beyond that mark — as far as it turns to give back as refreshment that which heaven dries from the sea, whence the rivers have what goes with them, virtue thus, as an enemy, flees from all like a serpent, either by the misfortune of the place or by bad usage that plunders them; hence the inhabitants of the wretched valley have so changed their nature that it seems Circe had them for pasture."
sentences.purgatorio-14-s11: Among filthy swine — more fit for gall than for any other food made for human use — it first directs its poor course.
sentences.purgatorio-14-s12: Then he encounters packs of hounds coming down, more snarling than their strength allows, and disdainfully turns his snout away from them.
sentences.purgatorio-14-s21: But since God wishes that so much of His grace should shine forth in you, I will not be sparing toward you; therefore know that I was Guido del Duca.
sentences.purgatorio-14-s22: My blood was so parched with envy that, if a man had seen someone become joyful, you would have seen me smeared with spite.
sentences.purgatorio-14-s23: From my seed I reap such straw; O human beings, why do you place your heart where the companionship of a spouse is forbidden?
sentences.purgatorio-14-s24: This is Rinier; this one is the pride and honor of the house of Calboli, where no deed has since borne an heir to his worth.
sentences.purgatorio-14-s33: Bagnacaval does well not to have offspring again; Castrocaro does ill, and Conio worse, who, by fathering such counts, only gets more entangled.
sentences.purgatorio-14-s34: The Pagans will do well, since the demon has turned away from them; but that does not mean that any pure testimony about them will ever remain.
sentences.purgatorio-14-s35: O Ugolin of the Fantolini, your name is secure, since no one any longer is expected who could do it, by deviating, and make it obscure.
sentences.purgatorio-14-s36: But go away now, Tuscan; for my mind now delights too much in weeping rather than in speaking, so our reason has tightly constrained my mind.
sentences.purgatorio-14-s29: When in Bologna does a smith grow sulky?
sentences.purgatorio-14-s30: When in Faenza a Bernardin, son of Fosco, (a) noble scion of little stock?
sentences.purgatorio-14-s31: Do not marvel if I weep, Tuscan, when I remember — with Guido da Prata, Ugolin son of Azzo who lived among us, Federigo Tignoso and his band, the house Traversara and the Anastagi (and one people and the other is...directed), the women and the knights, the troubles and the comforts that made us desire them — love and courtesy — where hearts are made so wicked.
sentences.purgatorio-14-s32: O Bretinoro, why don't you flee away, since your household has gone off and many people (have gone) so as not to be called wicked?
